summary: Cybercab driving itself out of the GigaTexas factory
ai_summary: Elon Musk shared a video of Tesla's Cybercab autonomous vehicle successfully driving itself out of the GigaTexas factory, demonstrating progress in autonomous driving technology and manufacturing integration. This represents a key milestone in Tesla's robotaxi program, showing the practical application of self-driving capabilities in real-world factory operations and highlighting advancement toward full vehicle autonomy—a critical business objective for Tesla's future revenue model.
embed_html:
Cybercab driving itself out of the GigaTexas factory pic.twitter.com/EwAMVVDjYy

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 28, 2026

ai_summary: SpaceX is developing a proprietary AI training infrastructure stack written in C that optimizes for their specific hardware configuration (220k GB300s GPUs with 800G network interconnects), prioritizing pipeline parallelism and bare-metal performance to potentially outperform existing frameworks like JAX for large-scale model training. This demonstrates a critical business strategy of vertical integration and custom infrastructure optimization—building in-house tools tailored to specific hardware constraints rather than relying on generic frameworks, which can yield significant performance gains for compute-intensive operations at massive scale.

parent.text: BREAKING: Massive explosion by Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket on a launchpad in Cape Canaveral, Florida Major setback for Jeff Bezos
parent.name: Visegrád 24
parent.embed_html:
BREAKING:

Massive explosion by Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket on a launchpad in Cape Canaveral, Florida

Major setback for Jeff Bezos pic.twitter.com/qTOpDxGXLB

— Visegrád 24 (@visegrad24) May 29, 2026

ai_summary: Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket experienced a catastrophic failure on the launch pad at Cape Canaveral, representing a major setback for Jeff Bezos's space venture. Elon Musk's response—'Rockets are hard'—acknowledges a fundamental business reality in aerospace: rocket development involves extreme technical complexity, high failure risk, and massive capital requirements, making it one of the most challenging engineering endeavors in the industry. This serves as a candid observation about the inherent difficulties and resource intensity required to compete in space exploration and commercial spaceflight, relevant to SpaceX's operational challenges and the competitive dynamics of the rocket launch market.
embed_html:
Most unfortunate. Rockets are hard.

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 29, 2026

ai_summary: The thread discusses SpaceX's potential to monetize its Colossus II AI compute infrastructure (being built for ~$17B) by leasing it to other companies, following xAI's model of leasing Colossus I to Anthropic for $45B over 3 years. Elon clarifies that SpaceX has not committed to multi-year leases and is instead offering flexible 180-day terms with 90-day mutual cancellation clauses—a strategic decision to maintain optionality while building trust with customers. This reveals how SpaceX is exploring new revenue streams in AI compute infrastructure beyond its core aerospace business.

There’s a ton of headroom to better optimize AI training and inference. https://t.co/1BTULo6x4I

— Matthew Prince 🌥 (@eastdakota) May 28, 2026

ai_summary: Elon discusses optimization opportunities in AI training infrastructure, specifically addressing how JAX and similar frameworks are optimized for Google's TPU environment rather than the massive-scale Nvidia GPU training runs required by his AI initiatives (likely xAI). The insight highlights a critical engineering challenge: existing open-source ML tools require significant re-optimization when scaling from medium to massive training runs, revealing both technical debt in current frameworks and competitive advantages available through custom infrastructure optimization.
embed_html:
Yes.

It’s not that we’ve discovered some magic bullet, but rather that JAX, or at least the open source version of it, is mostly optimized for small to medium-sized training runs on Google TPUs, whereas we need to massive training runs on Nvidia GPUs.

Pipeline parallelism is…
